title: Microwave resonance cavity
abstract: Microwave resonance cavities and associated methods and apparatus are described. In one example, a cavity (100) comprises a first and a second input port (102, 104) for inputting microwave radiation at a first and a second frequency respectively. The microwave radiation at the first frequency may be to excite a sample in the cavity whereas the microwave radiation at the second frequency may be to interrogate a sample in the cavity for analysis. The cavity has dimensions such that it resonates at both the first and the second frequency.
claims:
Claims Claims 1 A microwave resonance cavity comprising: a first input port for inputting microwave radiation at a first frequency, the microwave radiation at the first frequency being to excite a sample in the cavity; a second input port for inputting microwave radiation at a second frequency, the microwave radiation at the second frequency being to interrogate a sample in the cavity for analysis, wherein the cavity has dimensions such that it resonates at both the first frequency and the second frequency. A microwave resonance cavity as claimed in claim 1 wherein the excitation caused by the microwave radiation at the first frequency is heating. A microwave resonance cavity as claimed in claim 1 or claim 2, wherein the cavity has a sample receiving region and is dimensioned such that: the microwave radiation at the first frequency results in a high electric field in the sample receiving region; and the microwave radiation at the second frequency results in a high magnetic field in the sample receiving region. A microwave resonance cavity as claimed in claim 3 wherein the cavity is dimensioned such that the first frequency results in a TE101 resonant mode and the second frequency results in a resonant TE102 mode. 5. A microwave resonance cavity as claimed in claim 3 or claim 4 wherein the first input port is proximal to a maximum of the magnetic field of a resonant mode of the microwave radiation at the first frequency and the second input port is proximal to a maximum in the magnetic field of a resonant mode of the microwave radiation at the second frequency. 6 A microwave resonance cavity as claimed in any proceeding claim, wherein the second input port comprises a waveguide for connecting the cavity to a microwave analysis unit. 7. A microwave resonance cavity as claimed in claim 6 wherein the second input port is further configured to receive radiation output by the excited sample. A microwave resonance cavity as claimed in claims 6 or 7 wherein the waveguide has a cut-off frequency which is greater than the first frequency and less than the second frequency. A microwave resonance cavity as claimed in any proceeding claim wherein the first input port is a coaxial input port. A microwave resonance cavity as claimed in any proceeding claim wherein the cavity is an elliptic cylinder. A microwave resonance cavity as claimed in claims 9 to 10 wherein the elliptic cylinder comprises first and second ends and the cavity comprises two separable parts, the parts separable in a plane perpendicular to the ends of the elliptic cylinder. A microwave resonance cavity as claimed in any preceding claim wherein the elliptic cylinder is dimensioned such that the second frequency is an X-band microwave frequency. A method of performing microwave analysis in a microwave resonance cavity comprising: irradiating the microwave cavity at a first frequency to excite the sample; and irradiating a microwave resonance cavity at a second frequency to perform an interrogation of a sample; wherein the steps of irradiating the microwave resonance cavity to excite the sample and irradiating the microwave resonance cavity to perform an interrogation of the sample are carried out at least partially concurrently. 14. A method of performing microwave analysis according to claim 13 wherein: the first frequency excites the cavity at a mode having a maximum electric field at the location of the sample; and 

 the second frequency excites the cavity at a mode having a maximum magnetic field at the location of the sample. A method as claimed in claim 13 or 14 further comprising filtering the first frequency from an interrogation signal prior to analysis of the interrogating radiation. A method as claimed in any of claims 13 to 15 wherein performing the microwave radiation absorption analysis comprises measuring an electron paramagnetic resonance spectrum. 17. A method as claimed in any of claims 13 to 15, further comprising performing microwave resonance spectroscopy of the sample. 18. A method as claimed in claim 17, wherein the microwave resonance spectroscopy is electron paramagnetic resonance spectroscopy. 19. A method as claimed in claim 17, wherein the microwave resonance spectroscopy is nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy. 20. A method as claimed in any of claims 13 to 15, wherein the method further comprises determining a change in absorption of the radiation at the second frequency. 21. A method as claimed in claim 20 wherein the method further comprises applying a rotating magnetic field to the sample. 22. A method as claimed in claim 21 wherein the sample comprises magnetic nanoparticles. A method as claimed in claim 22 further comprising determining a rate of rotation of nanoparticles in the sample based on the determined change in absorption. 24. A method as claimed in claim 23 further comprising determining the presence of specific biological matter in a sample based on the rate of rotation of the nanoparticles. 25. A microwave resonance apparatus comprising: a microwave cavity with a first resonance mode at an exciting microwave frequency and a second resonance mode at an interrogating microwave frequency; an interrogation unit comprising an interrogating microwave source; and an exciting microwave source, wherein the interrogating microwave source and the exciting microwave source are configured to excite the cavity at a first and second resonant mode of the cavity respectively. 26. A microwave resonance apparatus as claimed in claim 25, wherein the microwave cavity is configured to receive a sample to be interrogated within the cavity. 27. A microwave resonance apparatus as claimed in claims 25 or 26, further comprising a waveguide, the waveguide being configured to supply interrogating microwaves from the interrogating microwave source to the microwave cavity. A microwave resonance apparatus as claimed in claim 27, wherein the interrogation unit further comprises a microwave analysis unit, wherein the waveguide is further to transmit a microwave return signal from the microwave cavity to the microwave analysis unit, wherein the waveguide is to filter radiation from the exciting microwave source from the return signal. A microwave resonance apparatus as claimed in claims 25 to 28 the cavity further comprising a coaxial input port configured to input exciting microwaves from the exciting microwave source to the microwave cavity. A microwave resonance apparatus as claimed in claims 25 to 29, wherein the microwave resonance apparatus further comprises a magnetic field source. 31 A microwave resonance apparatus as claimed in claim 30, wherein the magnetic field source is configured to provide a magnetic field for at least one of magnetic resonance spectroscopy or magnetic nanoparticle polarization analysis. 32. A method of performing microwave analysis comprising: locating a sample for analysis in a microwave resonance cavity; irradiating the microwave resonance cavity at a first frequency; and irradiating the microwave resonance cavity at a second frequency; wherein: the radiation at the first frequency is to excite the sample and radiation at the second frequency is for analysis of the sample. 33. A method of performing microwave analysis according to claim 32 wherein: the radiation at the first frequency has a maximum electric field at the location of the sample and the radiation at the second frequency has a maximum magnetic field at the location of the sample. 34. A method of performing microwave analysis as claimed in claim 32 or claim 33, wherein the sample remains within the cavity for both excitation by radiation at the first frequency and analysis by radiation at the second frequency. 35. A method of performing microwave analysis as claimed in claims 32 to 34, wherein the sample comprises magnetic nanoparticles and the method further comprises externally applying a time varying magnetic field to the sample in the cavity. 36. A method of performing microwave analysis as claimed in claim 35 wherein the magnetic field is a time varying field to induce a rotation in the nanoparticles in the sample. 37. A method of performing microwave analysis as claimed in claims 32 to 36, wherein the method further comprises measuring an absorption of microwave radiation at the second frequency. 38. A method of performing microwave analysis as claimed in claim 37, wherein the method further comprises determining a property of the sample based on the measured absorption of microwave radiation at the second frequency. 39. A method as claimed in claim 37 wherein the sample comprises nanoparticles and the method comprises using the change in absorption of microwave radiation at the second frequency to determine the rate of rotation of nanoparticles in the sample. 40. A method as claimed in claim 39 further comprising determining the presence of specific biological matter in a sample based on the rate of rotation of the nanoparticles. 41. A method as claimed in any of claims 32 to 34 which comprises microwave resonance spectroscopy. 42. A method as claimed in any of claims 32 to 41 comprising filtering the first frequency from the signal prior to analysis of the radiation.
